core.action: Use alpha=0.05 as the default confidence interval level (producing 95% CIs) — this aligns with scipy, statsmodels, and R survival package conventions for interoperability

consequence.description: Changing default alpha away from 0.05 alters statistical inference behavior, producing non-standard confidence intervals that differ from established survival analysis conventions

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-022

core.when: When handling tied event times in Cox proportional hazards models
core.action: Use Efron's method for handling ties in Cox PH model — Efron's method approximates the partial likelihood by averaging over tied event times, providing better accuracy than Breslow when tie fraction exceeds 5%

consequence.description: Using Breslow approximation instead of Efron's method reduces accuracy when multiple subjects fail at the same time, producing biased coefficient estimates in datasets with significant tied event times

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-023

core.when: When implementing censoring type detection and model fitting methods
core.action: Maintain consistent dispatch mechanism for censoring type detection — do not mix method-name string parsing (fit_right_censoring vs fit_left_censoring) with enum-as-decorator patterns (CensoringType); choose one paradigm and apply it consistently

consequence.description: Mixing string-based dispatch and enum-based state machines creates architectural inconsistency that confuses developers and risks refactoring bugs where one pattern is used where the other is expected

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-115

validation_threshold: uses_string_dispatch == True AND uses_enum_decorator == True → FAIL

core.when: When implementing or refactoring mixture cure model optimization in ParametricUnivariateFitter
core.action: Set cured fraction bounds to (0, 1) to constrain the cured fraction parameter within valid probability space — do not remove or relax bounds to (-0.1, 1.1) or unbounded

consequence.description: Without (0, 1) bounds, optimization may explore invalid probability spaces producing negative or exceeding-1 cured fractions that are mathematically impossible, corrupting model estimates

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-041

core.when: When estimating survival curves with CoxPHFitter using cluster_col for clustered or correlated survival times
core.action: Use Huber sandwich estimator for variance estimation instead of model-based variance — do not use naive variance estimation which underestimates uncertainty in clustered data

consequence.description: Naive variance underestimates standard errors for clustered survival times, leading to incorrect confidence intervals and potentially invalid statistical conclusions that could cause wrong treatment or investment decisions

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-055

core.when: When implementing or refactoring ParametricUnivariateFitter optimization routines
core.action: Use L-BFGS-B as the default optimization method — do not replace with unbounded methods like Nelder-Mead that cannot enforce parameter bounds required for valid distribution parameters (scale > 0, shape >= 0)

consequence.description: Unbounded optimizers may converge to invalid parameter values (negative scale, invalid shape) causing NaN or infinite likelihood values and completely invalid model fits

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-025

core.when: When implementing or refactoring SplineFitter for survival modeling
core.action: Use cubic splines with natural boundary conditions (linear extrapolation beyond boundary knots) — do not use polynomial splines with clamped boundaries which produce endpoint oscillations corrupting tail survival estimates

consequence.description: Polynomial splines with clamped boundaries exhibit oscillations at endpoints, producing physically implausible survival probability estimates near 0 or 1 and invalidating tail extrapolation

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-028

core.when: When implementing or refactoring iteration logic for event processing
core.action: Preserve adaptive batch/single iteration mode selection based on tie fraction threshold — use batch iteration for datasets with >1% tied events, single iteration for datasets with fewer ties

consequence.description: Removing adaptive tie-based mode selection causes either inefficient batch processing for untied data or incorrect results for high-tie datasets when single iteration mode is applied inappropriately

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-039

core.when: When implementing survival curve comparison with weighted log-rank test
core.action: Use default inverse-variance log-rank weights for proportional hazards testing; for early-diverging curves use Peto-Wilcoxon weights, for late-diverging curves use Tarone-Ware weights

consequence.description: Using unweighted or incorrectly weighted log-rank tests reduces statistical power for detecting survival differences, potentially missing significant risk factors in hazard-based comparisons

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-043

core.when: When configuring Cox proportional hazards model with non-smooth baseline hazard
core.action: Use piecewise constant baseline hazard only when the proportional hazards assumption is violated or when interval-specific hazard estimates are explicitly needed; for standard Cox PH analysis, use smooth baseline hazard models (e.g., Breslow estimator)

consequence.description: Piecewise constant baseline hazard models non-proportional hazards, producing fundamentally different hazard ratios than standard Cox PH; using it for proportional hazards data leads to unnecessarily complex models with interval-specific coefficients that may confuse interpretation

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-085

core.when: When using ParametricRegressionFitter to estimate survival models with heterogeneous covariate scales
core.action: Understand that the model normalizes covariates by standard deviation before optimization and then un-normalizes parameters for reporting; do not directly interpret coefficient magnitudes without accounting for this scaling

consequence.description: Without accounting for normalization, users misinterpret coefficient magnitudes as relative importance; a coefficient of 0.5 on a normalized covariate has different practical meaning than on a raw covariate, leading to incorrect investment or treatment decisions based on wrong parameter priorities

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-106

core.when: When running Kaplan-Meier survival estimation on clinical datasets with delayed patient enrollment
core.action: Explicitly provide entry_times parameter for each observation instead of relying on default zeros; if entry times are not available, acknowledge potential left-truncation bias in results interpretation

consequence.description: Default entry times of zero combined with Kaplan-Meier's assumption of immediate entry creates silent left-truncation bias; clinical studies with delayed enrollment produce systematically overestimated survival probabilities that cannot be reproduced in practice

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-108

core.when: When interpreting mixture cure model results for financial or clinical outcomes
core.action: Recognize that the model distinguishes two cure states labeled 'default' and 'repay' with distinct economic interpretations; do not collapse these into a single cured versus not-cured binary when making decisions about intervention strategies or risk scoring

consequence.description: Assuming a single cure state when the mixture cure model produces two distinct states leads to misinterpreting recovery pathways; conflating 'default' and 'repay' cure types causes incorrect risk stratification and inappropriate intervention targeting in credit or clinical settings

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-004

core.when: When implementing or calling the sample size calculation for Cox proportional hazards models
core.action: Use Schoenfeld's formula via lifelines.statistics.sample_size_necessary_under_cph — do not substitute Freedman's method or other approximation formulas without theoretical validation

consequence.description: Using incorrect sample size formulas produces wrong power calculations, leading to underpowered studies that fail to detect true effects or overpowered studies that waste resources

evidence_refs.summary: Derived from BD: BD-088

core.when: When fitting survival curves with KaplanMeierFitter without specifying a timeline parameter
core.action: Verify that the auto-generated timeline (sorted unique event times) matches the intended analysis granularity; specify explicit timeline if interpolation at arbitrary points or consistent time bucketing is required

consequence.description: Auto-generated timelines produce different survival estimates depending on data distribution, causing inconsistent results across datasets with varying event time patterns

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-006

core.when: When using qq_plot with parametric survival models (WeibullAFTFitter, ExponentialAFTFitter, etc.)
core.action: Verify the lifelines model class name has a valid scipy distribution equivalent before calling get_distribution_name_of_lifelines_model; check that the mapped scipy distribution is available in the scipy version in use

consequence.description: Invalid model-to-distribution mapping causes AttributeError or incorrect QQ plot visualization, obscuring whether the parametric model fits the data appropriately

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-020

core.when: When adding new parametric fitter classes to the lifelines library
core.action: Verify new fitter classes follow the three-level inheritance chain (BaseFitter->UnivariateFitter->ParametricUnivariateFitter) and update the model-to-scipy distribution mapping in get_distribution_name_of_lifelines_model

consequence.description: New parametric fitters without proper inheritance break polymorphic usage and QQ plot functionality, causing runtime errors in downstream analysis

core.when: When implementing or refactoring code that accesses CoxPHFitter attributes (hazard_, survival_function_, etc.)
core.action: Call fit() method before accessing any model attribute — CoxPHFitter enforces strict lifecycle contract via __getattr__ proxy that raises AttributeError on unfitted instances

consequence.description: Accessing model attributes before calling fit() triggers AttributeError at runtime, causing immediate execution failure; this pattern cannot be bypassed by checking for None or other sentinel values

core.when: When evaluating Cox model predictive performance using concordance index
core.action: Compute concordance index on predicted risk scores (linear predictor), not predicted survival times — C-index on times requires extrapolation and is not implemented; use risk scores for valid model comparison

consequence.description: Computing C-index on predicted survival times instead of risk scores requires extrapolation beyond observed data, producing invalid model evaluation that does not measure concordance correctly

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-016

core.when: When fitting Cox proportional hazards models with tied event times
core.action: Use Efron's approximation for handling tied event times — this method is more accurate than Breslow without O(n^2) complexity; note that exact marginal likelihood is only used when ties < 5% of data AND n > 500; below this threshold Efron is applied uniformly regardless of tie percentage

consequence.description: Using Breslow's method for ties instead of Efron's approximation introduces systematic bias in coefficient estimates; exact methods are prohibitively slow at O(n^2) complexity for large datasets

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-010

validation_threshold: tie_method == 'Breslow' → WARN; tie_method == 'exact' AND n > 500 → WARN

core.when: When using survival analysis with clinical or epidemiological data that may contain left-truncated observations
core.action: Verify that entry_times are explicitly provided for each observations; if not provided, assume entry=0 may introduce systematic bias in presence of left-truncation. Check for the presence of entry time columns and validate they are non-zero when the data source documents truncation.

consequence.description: Defaulting entry times to zero when left-truncation exists causes systematic underestimation of survival probabilities, leading to biased clinical conclusions and potentially flawed treatment recommendations

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-007

core.when: When implementing central tendency calculations for survival analysis summary statistics
core.action: Use mode for categorical variable central tendency and median for numeric variable central tendency — do not use mean as a universal central tendency measure

consequence.description: Using mean for categorical data produces meaningless statistics since categorical distributions lack numeric order; using mean for skewed continuous data underestimates central tendency, leading to incorrect survival probability estimates

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-060

core.when: When implementing model comparison logic for non-nested survival models
core.action: Use AIC with 2*k penalty for model comparison — do not replace with BIC or other information criteria

consequence.description: BIC with log(n)*k penalty tends to select underparameterized models that may underfit complex survival relationships, causing suboptimal model selection and poor predictive performance in live trading

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-061

core.when: When implementing Cox PH model optimization for survival regression with tied event times
core.action: Use Newton-Raphson with Efron tie correction for Cox PH estimation — do not replace with Breslow approximation

consequence.description: Breslow approximation underestimates variance in heavy-tie scenarios, causing confidence intervals to be too narrow and p-values to be spuriously significant, leading to incorrect feature selection in survival models

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-062

core.when: When implementing coefficient penalty regularization for high-dimensional Cox PH models
core.action: Use elastic net regularization combining L1 and L2 penalties — do not use pure LASSO (L1 only) or pure ridge (L2 only) penalty

consequence.description: Pure LASSO may arbitrarily select one feature from correlated groups while dropping others; pure ridge cannot perform variable selection; both lead to suboptimal feature selection and overfitting in high-dimensional survival data

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-063

core.when: When implementing survival curve estimation for right-censored event data
core.action: Use product-limit (Kaplan-Meier) estimator for right-censored survival estimation — do not replace with life-table method

consequence.description: Life-table method provides interval-level estimates that are less precise than individual-level product-limit estimates, causing loss of granularity in survival probability calculations and reducing the accuracy of time-to-event predictions

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-065

core.when: When using AalenAdditiveFitter for survival analysis
core.action: Normalize covariates to mean=0, std=1 before fitting; use denormalization step when interpreting coefficients in original scale; verify standardization was applied if comparing coefficients across models

consequence.description: Without proper normalization, Aalen additive model coefficients become incomparable due to different variable magnitudes, causing numerical instability and potentially misleading survival risk assessments

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-048

core.when: When initializing CRCSplineFitter for Cox regression with splines
core.action: Verify gamma0_=0.0 and gamma1_=2.0 are used as initial values; for datasets with extreme baseline hazards (very high or very low), adjust initialization to match the expected baseline hazard shape

consequence.description: Default gamma initialization assumes Gompertz-like baseline hazard; datasets with different hazard shapes may converge to biased coefficient estimates, causing systematic under or over-estimation of covariate effects

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-051

core.when: When configuring NPMLE convergence tolerance for interval-censored survival analysis
core.action: Verify tolerance is set to 1e-5 for standard applications; increase to 1e-7 for simulation studies requiring high precision; use 1e-3 only for quick exploratory diagnostics

consequence.description: Insufficient tolerance (1e-3) causes the NPMLE to converge prematurely, producing imprecise survival estimates with systematic errors that compound across long event-time horizons

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-052

core.when: When implementing or refactoring survival analysis power calculations
core.action: Implement power calculation using the Cox proportional hazards analytical formula as provided in lifelines/statistics.py:power_under_cph — do not replace with simulation-based power unless explicitly requested by the user

consequence.description: Replacing analytical power calculation with simulation-based approach changes computation characteristics, causing slower execution and inconsistent results across different hardware configurations

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-089

core.when: When implementing or refactoring model comparison logic in parametric survival fitters
core.action: Use AIC and BIC information criteria for formal comparison of non-nested survival models — implement formulas in lifelines/fitters/__init__.py:AIC_ and verify both criteria are available for model selection

consequence.description: Omitting AIC/BIC forces users to rely on likelihood ratio tests which cannot handle non-nested models with different parameter counts, leading to incorrect model selection and poor statistical inference

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-091

core.when: When optimizing gradient computation for Cox proportional hazards Efron approximation
core.action: Implement adaptive selection between batch and single-threaded Efron gradient computation based on problem size — small datasets must use single-threaded computation for efficiency, large datasets must use batch processing to manage memory

consequence.description: Using fixed batch size across all datasets causes memory overflow on large datasets or unnecessary overhead on small datasets, resulting in either crashes or degraded performance

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-096

core.when: When implementing or refactoring any fit method in the lifelines fitters module
core.action: Begin every fit method with df=df.copy() to create an isolated copy of the input DataFrame — this defensive copying pattern must be applied at each 24+ fit entry points to prevent external mutations from affecting the fitter's internal state

consequence.description: Removing defensive copying allows external DataFrame mutations to propagate into the fitter, causing subtle reproducibility issues where the same data produces different results depending on prior operations

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-099

core.when: When implementing or refactoring parameter bounds for exponential-family distributions in survival models
core.action: Apply _MIN_PARAMETER_VALUE=1e-9 as the universal lower bound floor for each parametric distributions via the _buffer_bounds function — parameters must never be allowed to reach zero which causes log(0) singularities and infinite gradients

consequence.description: Allowing parameters to approach zero causes log(0) singularities and infinite gradients in exponential-family distributions, leading to optimization failures and NaN values in model estimates

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-103

core.when: When interpreting AalenAdditiveFitter coefficients after model fitting
core.action: Denormalize coefficients by original standard deviations to restore them to original covariate scales, enabling direct interpretation of covariate effects on the additive hazard scale

consequence.description: Skipping denormalization produces uninterpretable coefficients on arbitrary scales, leading to incorrect conclusions about covariate effects and potentially causing adoption of invalid survival models

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-054

core.when: When using non-integer weights in survival model fitting
core.action: Specify robust=True to enable robust variance estimation; non-integer weights indicate sampling weights or propensity scores that require robust inference

consequence.description: Non-integer weights without robust variance estimation produce invalid statistical inference with underestimated standard errors, leading to false significance claims and incorrect model selection

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-056

core.when: When validating cumulative hazard estimates from non-known parametric models
core.action: Validate that cumulative hazard is non-decreasing; decreasing hazards are mathematically impossible and indicate modeling errors that must be corrected

consequence.description: Silent modeling errors producing decreasing cumulative hazards create mathematically impossible results, corrupting downstream survival probability calculations and leading to invalid risk assessments

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-057

core.when: When computing log-likelihood for model comparison using information criteria
core.action: Normalize log-likelihood by total weight sum to produce scale-invariant values comparable across datasets with different sample sizes or weighting schemes

consequence.description: Unnormalized log-likelihood makes AIC/BIC comparisons invalid across datasets with different weight totals, causing incorrect model selection and potentially adopting inferior survival models

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-058

core.when: When performing subtract/divide operations on survival functions
core.action: Use forward fill interpolation for survival function arithmetic operations to maintain mathematical coherence; this assumes constant hazard between observed time points and preserves valid probability ranges

consequence.description: Alternative interpolation methods like linear interpolation can produce negative survival probabilities, corrupting survival analysis results and causing invalid risk calculations in downstream applications

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-059

core.when: When implementing PnL tracking in backtesting
core.action: Assume the framework implements PnL conservation (realized + unrealized) — the framework does not have this capability; realized and unrealized PnL are tracked separately without conservation validation

consequence.description: Without PnL conservation, discrepancies between realized and unrealized PnL go undetected, causing equity miscalculation that compounds over time and produces unreliable backtest results

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-012

core.when: When implementing equity and PnL calculation logic
core.action: Implement PnL conservation check: after each trade event, verify that (current_equity - initial_equity) equals (cumulative_realized_pnl + current_unrealized_pnl) within tolerance of 0.001. Log discrepancy as ERROR and halt execution if mismatch exceeds tolerance

consequence.description: Without conservation validation, PnL tracking errors accumulate silently; equity curve diverges from actual returns, causing backtest results to be unreliable for live trading decisions

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-012

core.when: When implementing train/test split logic for model validation
core.action: Assume the framework enforces train/test time split integrity — the framework does not validate that test data timestamps are strictly after train data timestamps

consequence.description: Without temporal split validation, future data may leak into training set, causing look-ahead bias that makes backtest results appear significantly better than live performance

evidence_refs.summary: 7a8fc34 Derived from BD: BD-GAP-013

core.when: When implementing data preprocessing for model training
core.action: Validate temporal split integrity: verify that max(training_set.timestamp) is strictly less than min(test_set.timestamp). Reject with ERROR if temporal overlap exists; add explicit data_leakage_check flag to split configuration

consequence.description: Data leakage from test into training causes overfitted models that perform well in backtesting but fail in live trading, leading to direct financial losses

core.action: Assume the framework maintains an immutable event log — the framework does not implement append-only logging with tamper detection; events can be modified or deleted after recording

consequence.description: Without immutable logging, audit records can be altered post-hoc, compromising regulatory compliance and making it impossible to reconstruct exact sequence of trading decisions

core.action: Implement immutable event log: store each events with timestamp, content hash, and previous_event_hash (blockchain-style chaining). Append-only operations only; implement integrity verification function that recomputes hash chain and reports tampering

core.action: Assume the framework implements a rebalancing trigger mechanism — the framework does not have built-in drift detection or threshold-based rebalancing triggers

core.action: Implement rebalancing trigger mechanism: calculate weight_drift = abs(current_weight - target_weight) for each asset. Trigger rebalancing when max(weight_drift) exceeds threshold (default 5%) or when scheduled interval elapses
